options.content:
# W&B Run Data (history read path)

Use this when a customer says **"my run charts won't load, or show the wrong/missing data."** This is the **time-series READ path** — the code that fetches logged metric history out of the datastore (ClickHouse and the legacy Bigtable-style store) and hands it to the workspace charts.

**Two very different failure shapes — decide which one you're seeing first:**
- **SLOW data** — charts eventually load but take a long time. Look at **Read latency**.
- **WRONG or MISSING data** — charts load fast but points are missing, flat, or disagree with what the user expects. Look at **Fallbacks & correctness** and **Sampling adequacy** — the datastore returned an answer, it was just the wrong one.

Most DogStatsD panels here are per-namespace application metrics; the trace-derived latency panel is sampled. Use the **Open Traces in Explore** link (top-right) to jump into a slow read trace. For the underlying datastore health (ClickHouse cluster, disk, etc.) see the [W&B Datastores](/d/wandb-datastores) dashboard.

panels.title: ClickHouse read latency vs baseline
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). ch_hard_read_ch_latency_ms / ch_hard_read_base_latency_ms are filtered {namespace="$namespace"}. If these are exported as histograms rather than gauges, switch the avg to sum(rate(<name>_sum[...]))/sum(rate(<name>_count[...])).

options.content:
**What it shows** — how long the history read path takes to fetch metric points from ClickHouse (`ch`) versus the legacy baseline store, in milliseconds.

**What bad looks like** — ClickHouse latency sustained in the multi-hundred-ms to seconds range, or ClickHouse far above the baseline line. Brief spikes under load are normal.

**Do next** — if latency is the problem, this is SLOW data, not wrong data. Confirm with the span p95 panel below, then check the ClickHouse cluster on the [W&B Datastores](/d/wandb-datastores) dashboard. Grab a slow trace via **Open Traces in Explore** (top-right).

panels.title: History-reader span latency p95 (traces, sampled)
panels.description: Trace-derived (traces_spanmetrics_*, confirmed present) so it is SAMPLED — trust the shape, not exact values. The span_name regex is a best guess; if this panel is empty, run label_values(traces_spanmetrics_duration_milliseconds_bucket, span_name) and adjust the regex to the history-reader / clickhouse span names on your stack.

options.content:
**What it shows** — p95 duration of the read-path spans (history reader / ClickHouse query) from sampled traces, split by span.

**What bad looks like** — one span's p95 climbing into seconds, or a new span appearing at a specific minute.

**Do next** — note the slow span and the minute it started, then **Open Traces in Explore** (top-right) and read the actual span chain. If the panel is empty, the span-name regex needs adjusting (see the panel description).

panels.title: Hard-read fallbacks & shadow-read disagreements
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). ch_hard_read_success / ch_hard_read_fallback / ch_shadow_read_error / ch_shadow_read_empty / ch_shadow_read_len_diff / ch_shadow_read_not_enabled are all filtered {namespace="$namespace"}.

options.content:
**What it shows** — how often the read path fell back off ClickHouse (`fallback`), and where the shadow read (ClickHouse compared against the trusted store) disagreed: `error`, `empty`, or `length diff`.

**What bad looks like** — any sustained rate of `fallback`, `shadow error`, or `shadow length diff`. A fallback or a shadow-read diff means the newer store disagreed with the trusted store, so what the user sees may be WRONG or MISSING data.

**Do next** — this is a correctness signal, not a quick fix. Take a snapshot of this panel (with the time range) and **escalate** — note which signal is firing and the affected namespace. Cross-check dual-write below.

panels.title: Dual-write & replica-read activity
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). dual_write / replica_read are filtered {namespace="$namespace"}.

options.content:
**What it shows** — how much traffic is going through the dual-write migration path (writing to both stores) and reading from the replica.

**What bad looks like** — dual-write dropping to zero while reads still hit ClickHouse can mean the two stores are drifting apart; a sudden change in either line usually lines up with a migration config change.

**Do next** — use this as context for the fallback/shadow-read panel above. If those show disagreement and dual-write is off or partial, that is likely the cause — snapshot both and escalate with the namespace and time range.

panels.title: Sampling adequacy classification
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). gorilla_bigtablev2_sampling_adequate_normal / gorilla_bigtablev2_sampling_insufficient_intentional / gorilla_bigtablev2_sampling_insufficient_accidental / gorilla_bigtablev2_queried_no_samples are filtered {namespace="$namespace"}.

options.content:
**What it shows** — how the read path classified each query's sample coverage. `intentional` insufficiency is expected downsampling for zoomed-out views; `accidental` insufficiency and `queried, no samples` are not.

**What bad looks like** — any sustained `insufficient (accidental)` or `queried, no samples`. Accidental undersampling = charts silently missing points, so the user sees WRONG or incomplete data without any error.

**Do next** — if `accidental` or `no samples` is firing, snapshot this panel and **escalate** with the namespace and time range. Confirm against the ratio/counts panel below.

panels.title: Undersample ratio & sample counts
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). gorilla_bigtablev2_undersample_ratio / gorilla_bigtablev2_raw_rows_ratio / gorilla_bigtablev2_undersamples / gorilla_bigtablev2_samples_requested / gorilla_bigtablev2_samples_actual are filtered {namespace="$namespace"}. Related raw metrics: gorilla_bigtablev2_raw_rows_expected, gorilla_bigtablev2_raw_rows_actual, gorilla_bigtablev2_samples_attempted.

options.content:
**What it shows** — the raw numbers behind sampling: the undersample ratio, how far actual rows fell short of expected (`raw rows ratio`), and requested vs actual sample counts.

**What bad looks like** — undersample ratio climbing, `raw rows ratio` well below 1.0 (fewer rows came back than expected), or `samples actual` sitting far under `samples requested`.

**Do next** — pair this with the classification panel to the left. If both point at accidental undersampling, snapshot both and **escalate** with the namespace and time range.
